data.title: Malaysia Rubber Sector May Continue to Face Structural Headwinds — Market Talk
data.description: Malaysia's rubber products sector may continue to face persistent structural headwinds, RHB Investment Bankanalyst Eddy Do and his team say in a note. Oversupply, stiff global competition and weak pricing power are expected to weigh on its earnings outlook, they say. The sector's recent gains appear weak and driven more by market sentiment than fundamental improvement, they add. RHB prefers manufacturers with greater exposure to non-generic products such as cleanroom gloves. RHB maintains an underweight view on the sector, and names Kossan Rubber Industries as its top pick. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Kasikornbank's Bull Raises Earnings Projections on Strong 3Q — Market Talk
data.description: Kasikornbank's bull at UOB Kay Hian raises earnings forecasts for the Thai bank by 7.5% for 2025, 5.4% for 2026, and 4.6% for 2027 to reflect its stronger-than-expected 3Q earnings. Analyst Thanawat Thangchadakorn says in a research report that the bank likely adopted a neutral tone at an analyst meeting. Management mentioned the bank will prioritize quality by selectively lending and will focus on cost management and productivity improvement, the analyst notes. The bank has also set aside adequate provisions to cushion against future uncertainties. UOB KH raises the stock's target price to THB200.00 from THB192.00 with an unchanged buy rating. Shares last closed at THB180.50. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Japan's Consumer Inflation Picks Up, But Rate Hike Timing Still Unclear — Update
data.description:
By Megumi Fujikawa

TOKYO--A pickup in Japan's consumer inflation could buoy rate-hike hopes, but the central bank may tread cautiously as it waits for the newly formed Takaichi administration to settle in.

Data on Friday showed that consumer inflation--excluding volatile fresh food prices--increased 2.9% in September from a year earlier. That compared with August's 2.7% rise, and matched expectations of economists polled by data provider Quick.

As Japan experiences sticky price growth, differences of opinion have started to emerge within the central bank about the risks posed by inflation.

At its last meeting in September, two board members proposed raising interest rates to 0.75% from the current 0.5%, but were defeated by a majority vote.

Still, most economists and investors expect the central bank to keep interest rates on hold at its meeting next week.

The country's freshly picked prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, has said that the government should ultimately be responsible for the nation's economic policies, emphasizing the importance of close coordination with the BOJ.

Policymakers at the BOJ likely want more time to engage with the new political administration, and see more signs of economic strength, analysts say.

The latest flash purchasing managers index data for Japan pointed to a further loss of growth momentum across the private sector in October, said Annabel Fiddes, economics associate director at S&P Global Market Intelligence.

The S&P data indicated that Japan's manufacturing sector stayed stuck in decline, and while services remained a key growth engine for the wider economy, Fiddes warned that fading momentum there is a point of concern to monitor in the coming months.

Capital Economics' sense is that there is still a strong case to resume monetary tightening.

"However, with policymakers still keen to gather more information on the health of the economy, we suspect the next rate hike will only take place in January," said Abhijit Surya, senior APAC economist.

The central bank's messaging has remained cautious in recent weeks, and board members are concerned about the impact of U.S. tariffs on the economy, and spillovers to corporate profits and wage growth, he said in a note.

Write to Megumi Fujikawa at megumi.fujikawa@wsj.com

data.title: US warns it could force some Pennsylvania rail cars out of service
data.description:
Key points:
  • NTSB says Pennsylvania's Silverliner IV railcars pose fire risk
  • Governor says Transportation Secretary Duffy should push for more funding
  • Duffy says USDOT could take further action without reforms

By David Shepardson

U.S. Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy late on Thursday warned that the department could soon force a Pennsylvania transit agency to stop using a fleet of railcars due to fire risks as he raised broad safety and financial concerns.

The Federal Railroad Administration on October 1 issued an emergency order requiring the Southeastern Pennsylvania Transportation Authority to address fire risks within 30 days from its Silverliner IV railcars after an urgent safety recommendation by the National Transportation Safety Board following a series of five fires.

"SEPTA has not demonstrated sufficient capacity on its own to mitigate these significant safety and fiscal concerns," Duffy wrote in a letter to Pennsylvania Governor Josh Shapiro.

"If changes are not made immediately, it is only a matter of time before SEPTA's crumbling commuter rail system erupts in flames and kills someone." The rail system serves millions of riders yearly in the Philadelphia area.

SEPTA is the sixth-largest public transit system in the U.S. It provides service to five counties in the Greater Philadelphia area and connects to transit systems in Delaware and New Jersey.

Duffy noted that Philadelphia will host the FIFA World Cup in 2026, and "SEPTA's rail and bus systems must be ready to serve tens of thousands of additional guests safely."

A spokeswoman for Shapiro said the governor "has been fighting for additional recurring revenue to support SEPTA for the last two years – and as part of last year's budget, he secured $46 million in new funding for SEPTA."

She added Shapiro has sought another $167 million for SEPTA but state Senate Republicans have opposed the plan.

"Instead of issuing a press release, if Secretary Duffy actually wants to be helpful, he should call his fellow Republicans and get them to fund the Governor’s mass transit funding package for SEPTA," she added.

SEPTA has 225 Silverliner IV railcars that are around 50 years old and represent two-thirds of its regional rail fleet but for financial reasons must keep using the cars.

Halting use of the cars would require a two-thirds cut in service and would cost $2 billion but the authority is in dire funding straits, SEPTA said.

The cars are some of the oldest in the country and the agency has developed a comprehensive set of 40 mitigation measures, including additional notifications and safety checks to personnel and audible alarms for fault lights.

data.title: Heritage Commerce Corp reports results for the quarter ended September 30 - Earnings Summary
data.description:
• Heritage Commerce Corp reported quarterly adjusted earnings of 24 cents​​ per share for the quarter ended September 30, higher than the same quarter last year, when the company reported EPS of 17 cents. The mean expectation of six analysts for the quarter was for earnings of 21 cents per share. Wall Street expected results to range from 20 cents to 22 cents per share.

  • Revenue rose 17.2% to $46.79 million from a year ago; analysts expected $47.75 million.

  • Heritage Commerce Corp's reported EPS for the quarter was 24 cents​.

  • The company reported quarterly net income of $14.7 million.

  • Heritage Commerce Corp shares had fallen by 0.4% this quarter and gained 5.4% so far this year.

FORECAST CHANGES

  • The mean earnings estimate of analysts had risen by about 1.9% in the last three months.​

  • In the last 30 days, there have been no earnings estimate revisions by analysts covering the company.

RECOMMENDATIONS

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 6 "strong buy" or "buy," no "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell." The average consensus recommendation for the banks peer group is also "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Heritage Commerce Corp is $11.00, about 10.1% above its last closing price of $9.89

This summary was machine generated from LSEG data October 24 at 01:31 a.m. UTC. ​All figures in US dollars unless otherwise stated. data.title: Dollar calm as traders brace for US inflation data
data.description:
Key points:
  • US CPI due to be released on Friday despite government shutdown
  • Data unlikely to sway the Fed from cutting rates next week
  • Japan inflation above BOJ target, keeps rate hike hopes alive
  • Currencies muted ahead of crucial Trump-Xi meeting next week

By Ankur Banerjee

The U.S. dollar was steady on Friday, poised to eke out a small weekly gain against major rivals as investors braced for delayed inflation data that is unlikely to deter the Federal Reserve from lowering interest rates next week.

The Japanese yen was flat at 152.58 per U.S. dollar after weakening in the previous session as data showed Japan's core consumer prices stayed above the central bank's 2% target, keeping alive expectations of a near-term rate hike.

Beyond central bank meetings in Japan and the U.S. next week, investor focus is also on the looming meeting between U.S. President Donald Trump and Chinese President Xi Jinping, leaving the currency market fairly subdued on the day.

The Trump-Xi meeting next week in South Korea has spurred some expectations of a resolution to the escalating trade war between the world's top two economies.

"If President Trump's meeting with President Putin is any guide, we expect at least some issues will remain unresolved," said Joseph Capurso, head of international economics at the Commonwealth Bank of Australia.

"The best outcome is another trade truce in our view... We have low expectations of a positive outcome from the meeting. We judge market expectations are low too, implying only a modest reaction from currencies next week."

US INFLATION DATA AT LAST

Investors are watching out for the U.S. Consumer Price Index (CPI) for September due later on Friday, expecting a 0.4% increase for the headline number and a 0.3% rise in the core figure month-on-month.

The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics said last week it would publish the inflation report despite the government shutdown - now in its fourth week - to assist the Social Security Administration with its annual cost-of-living adjustment for 2026 for millions of retirees and other benefits recipients.

While analysts do not expect the data to hinder the Fed's path to cut rates next week by 25 basis points, it could provide cues on what the central bank might do in its December meeting.

Traders are almost fully pricing in a rate cut next week and another one at the December meeting.

"It would take a meaningful surprise to the upside for the market to change its mind about an additional interest rate cut by the Fed," said Julien Lafargue, chief market strategist at Barclays Private Bank.

The euro was little changed at $1.16195 in early trading but on course for a nearly 0.3% drop for the week. Sterling was flat at $1.3331, headed for a 0.8% weekly decline.

The dollar index , which measures the U.S. currency against six other units, was set for a 0.37% rise for the week. It was last at 98.92.

TAKING STOCK OF NEW SANCTIONS

New U.S. sanctions on major Russian suppliers Rosneft and Lukoil over Russia's war in Ukraine sent oil prices surging higher, following British sanctions on the same two companies last week.

That weighed on the currencies tied to oil imports including the yen. The yen's fate is also tied to the policies of Japan's new Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi, widely viewed as a fiscal and monetary dove.

Takaichi is preparing an economic stimulus package that is likely to exceed last year's $92 billion to help households tackle inflation, government sources familiar with the plan told Reuters on Wednesday.

That may prove to be a hurdle for the Bank of Japan to hike interest rates next week, with traders ascribing only a 19% chance of an increase.

"Given the Takaichi administration wants close coordination between the government and the BOJ, the BOJ may refrain from hiking interest rates until the economic package ordered by Takachi has been passed through the parliament," said CBA's Capurso.

data.title: Spot crude premiums jump as US sanctions on Russian producers drive China, India demand
data.description:
Key points:
  • Mideast benchmark Dubai premium more than doubled to 3-week high
  • Similar jumps seen in Oman, Murban premiums
  • China, India to buy more oil from Iraq, West Africa, Brazil

By Siyi Liu and Florence Tan

Spot premiums in crude markets jumped on Thursday on expectations that U.S. sanctions on top Russian producers will spur China and India's demand for supplies from the Middle East, Africa and South America, trade sources and analysts said.

Washington hit major suppliers Rosneft and Lukoil with sanctions over the Ukraine war, sparking concerns over tighter supply of oil from Russia, the top supplier to China and India. Global oil benchmark Brent futures rose by more than 4% on Thursday.

Indian refiners and some Chinese companies, buyers in the world's top importers, are set to curtail Russian oil imports to comply with the new sanctions, sources said, turning to other countries for alternative supply.

That sparked a jump in spot premiums for key Middle Eastern benchmarks on Thursday after slumping earlier this month on ample supply as the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries and their allies are increasing output.

Cash Dubai's premium settled at a three-week high of $2.71 per barrel, more than double the $1.26 per barrel of the previous session, Reuters data showed. It hit a 22-month low on October 2.

Spot premiums for other benchmark grades GME Oman and IFAD Murban also jumped to one-month highs at $3.12 and $2.86 a barrel, respectively, the data showed.

Privately-owned Reliance Industries will stop importing oil under a long-term deal to buy nearly 500,000 barrels per day of crude from Russian oil major Rosneft, two sources with direct knowledge of the matter said on Thursday.

Indian state refiners including Indian Oil Corp , Bharat Petroleum Corp and Hindustan Petroleum Corp are also reviewing their Russian oil trade documents to ensure no supply will be coming directly from Rosneft and Lukoil after the U.S. sanctioned the oil companies, a source with direct knowledge of the matter said.

In recent days, Reliance has purchased spot crude cargoes from Brazil and the Middle East, including Qatari al-Shaheen and Land grades, Iraqi Basra Medium, which could be used to partly replace Russian supplies, traders said. It was seen in the market on Thursday scouting for supplies, said a Middle Eastern trader approached by Reliance.

"We expect most substitute crudes will be sourced from the Middle East. The urgent need for sour barrels should enable the current Basra overhang to clear faster than we previously anticipated," said Richard Jones, a crude analyst at Energy Aspects.

"Today’s rally in Dubai has left Brent-Dubai swap-swap trading deeper in negative territory, supporting Atlantic basin arbs to Asia."

Brent's premium to Dubai quotes (DUB-EFS-1M) was at 1 cent a barrel on Thursday, having turned negative since the start of this week, LSEG data showed. A narrowing of the price gap makes Brent-linked grades from the Atlantic Basin more attractive for buyers in Asia.

Rosneft and Lukoil were also sanctioned by Britain last week.

data.title: Yomiuri: MUFG Looking to Expand U.S. Business, Executive Says
data.description:
By Kentaro Otsuka

Yomiuri Shimbun Staff Writer

Mitsubishi UFJ Financial Group Inc. (MUFG) celebrated its 20th anniversary on Oct. 1, and over the years it has proved highly profitable. That is thanks to major decisions in the massive U.S. market, such as to invest in Morgan Stanley and sell MUFG Union Bank.

Masatoshi Komoriya, a managing executive officer at MUFG who currently oversees the group's global commercial banking business, spoke about the decisions during an interview with The Yomiuri Shimbun. The following is excerpted from the interview.

The Yomiuri Shimbun: Amid the global economic turmoil triggered by the collapse of Lehman Brothers, you decided to invest about 900 billion yen in Morgan Stanley.

Masatoshi Komoriya: We announced a basic agreement for the investment on Sept. 22, 2008. In fact, the due diligence (DD) leading up to it was extremely short, taking only a few days. I was responsible for coordinating schedules and other matters within the DD team. (Before the announcement) we focused on interviewing key officials, and after the announcement, we conducted an additional review to confirm whether the closing conditions were met.

Although MUFG was already in the securities business, we felt that it would not be easy for us to grow the business significantly with our own securities franchise. We thought we couldn't compete with major Japanese securities firms without strengthening direct financing. In that context, (the investment in Morgan Stanley) was a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity.

The global economy was extremely turbulent at the time. From my perspective on the ground, the overriding feeling was simply a desire for the world to stabilize, rather than there being a focus on the exact percentage or amount to invest. Ultimately, we made the investment on Oct. 13, which was Monday and Columbus Day, by handing over a check.

Yomiuri: Why did you use a check for such a large investment?

Komoriya: On Columbus Day, banks were closed. But the stock market was open. It seemed that Morgan Stanley had told us they needed funds on hand by Monday morning. However, that day was also a holiday in Japan. The Zengin System (which handles interbank transactions) was not available. As a last resort, we used a check.

But then came the next hurdle: The check clearing houses in New York were closed. So, we searched for somewhere open and found a clearing house in (the adjacent state of) New Jersey that was operating. We were then concerned about sending just one person to deliver it to Morgan Stanley, so two others also went. I heard that the next day, Katsunori Nagayasu, president (of MUFG's banking unit at the time), went to Morgan Stanley's head office and received a standing ovation.

Yomiuri: What do you think were the benefits of investing in and partnering with Morgan Stanley?

Komoriya: We have a corporate customer base of about 2,000 companies in the United States, excluding Japanese-affiliated firms. While U.S. banks tightened project finance as they faced tougher regulations on capital in the wake of the 2008 financial crisis, we kept steady. As a result, there were more than 10 years in which we topped U.S. league tables for project finance (rankings of deal values).

As deals grow larger, it becomes difficult to arrange everything in-house on your own, necessitating partnerships with other financial institutions. Having Morgan Stanley as a collaborator allows us to quickly form a team and enter large-scale projects. Sometimes we initiate the deal, and sometimes it comes from them. Although Japan-U.S. relations have been in the spotlight recently, within the business community, I believe the collaboration between MUFG and Morgan Stanley is widely recognized as an excellent partnership.

Yomiuri: Regarding the sale of MUFG Union Bank in 2021, when did internal discussions begin, and what form did they take?

Komoriya: We were aiming to expand operations across the United States in wholesale and retail banking, but MUFG Union Bank, being a regional bank originating in California, did not have a large customer base or system infrastructure. We made several small acquisitions around 2010 to gain scale, but it was not enough to strengthen those foundations.

U.S. banks gradually recovered from their struggles in the wake of the financial crisis and began investing in mobile banking from around the mid-2010s. During that time, MUFG Union Bank grew less competitive in comparative terms as it wasn't large and it failed to drastically overhaul its outdated systems. It wasn't so much that the bank collapsed, but rather that the gap widened as others strengthened their digital capabilities.

The various directives we received from regulators, indicating for example that the bank's IT infrastructure was weak, were a major factor in the decision to sell. It wasn't like the bank's lending and deposit operations were collapsing, but they weren't growing either. My understanding is that the sale was decided under the leadership of (MUFG President and Group CEO Hironori) Kamezawa.

Yomiuri: Was there any momentum before the sale to reinvest in IT and turn things around?

Komoriya: It's true we considered various options in the 2010s. But as MUFG Bank Ltd.'s branch system in the United States also had vulnerabilities, we prioritized strengthening that first. Since it was thought that it would take several years for system projects, we had to prioritize.

Yomiuri: Is there a possibility of restarting the U.S. retail business that was lost with the sale of MUFG Union Bank?

Komoriya: Looking at the situation broadly, I think no one doubts how deep the U.S. financial market is. Strengthening our business in Japan, where interest rates have returned, is also an important theme. However, continuing to build pillars of revenue overseas is extremely important, and the United States is particularly crucial.

Personally, I believe that though we sold MUFG Union Bank, we don't need to abandon options outside of wholesale banking. There are challenges with regulatory compliance and dealing with authorities, but we can leverage our experience from that bank. It's not that we're thinking that we won't do retail banking; rather, we are seriously considering how to expand our business operations in the United States.

----

This article is from The Yomiuri Shimbun. Neither Dow Jones Newswires, MarketWatch, Barron's nor The Wall Street Journal were involved in the creation of this content.

YDN-M0000152580-1

data.title: India rates rise from over 9-year low, Thai prices remain subdued on flat demand
data.description:
Key points:
  • Thai prices hit lowest since October 2007
  • Flat demand, ample supply keep a lid on Thai prices -trader
  • India prices edge up from 9-year low
  • Bangladesh to import 50,000 tons white rice from Myanmar

By Sherin Elizabeth Varghese

Indian rice exports edged up from a more than nine-year low this week, supported by a stronger rupee despite muted demand, while Thai prices extended declines for a sixth consecutive week, hitting an 18-year low on weak buying activity.

Very few export deals were signed in India this week, as buyers are not in a hurry to make purchases due to falling prices, said a Kolkata-based trader.

India's 5% broken parboiled variety (RI-INBKN5-P1) was quoted this week at $344-$350 per ton, up from last week's $340-$345. Indian 5% broken white rice was priced at $360 to $370 per ton this week. Last week, prices hit their lowest since May 2016.

Thailand's 5% broken rice (RI-THBKN5-P1) was quoted at $337 per tonne, steady from $335–$340 last week, its lowest since October 2007.

Traders say demand has been flat with no major deals for Thai rice this week. Supply has also been ample which has helped maintain prices at this level, a Bangkok-based trader said.

"Slight price fluctuation now comes mainly from the exchange rate," the trader added.

Vietnam's 5% broken rice (RI-VNBKN5-P1) was offered at $420–$435 per ton on Thursday, unchanged from last week, at a near two-month low, according to the Vietnam Food Association.

"Domestic trading activity is quiet as many exporters have slowed down their purchases of paddy rice from farmers due to weak external demand," said a trader based in Ho Chi Minh City.

This has led to a decline in the prices of fresh unhusked paddy by 200 dong to 300 dong per kg over the past few days, traders said.

Vietnam exported 201,416 tons of rice in the first half of October, down 47% from the same period last year, according to the government's customs data.

Meanwhile, Bangladesh is importing rice to curb rising prices, buying 50,000 metric tons of white rice from Myanmar at $376.50 a ton under a government deal, and another 50,000 tons of parboiled rice through an international tender at $355.99 a ton.

data.title: DeFi perps volume explodes past $1T in a record month so far
data.description:
Decentralized perpetual trading volume is set for a massive month in October, having just passed a record $1 trillion with a week still to spare as traders place big bets on the crypto markets.

The $1 trillion milestone has already beaten August’s record of $762 billion by a considerable margin, according to data from DeFiLlama.

While Hyperliquid leads October with $317.6 billion in trading volume, Lighter, Aster, and edgeX have also fared well, seeing $255.4 billion, $177.6 billion and $134.7 billion, respectively, with the rest made up of smaller decentralized exchanges.

Those platforms contributed to a record $78 billion in decentralized perps volume on Oct. 10 alone. 

Given the current run rate, decentralized perps volume is on track to finish October at about $1.3 trillion — nearly doubling August’s tally.

Perps have become popular due to their 24/7 trading, high leverage, no expiration, and the ability to profit from both rising and falling markets — attracting speculative traders seeking higher returns with minimal holding requirements.

CEXs are still perps kings, but DEXs are catching up

Decentralized perps trading volume remains a fraction of that seen on centralized exchanges, with Binance and Bybit seeing $69.3 billion and $26 billion in trading volume over the last 24 hours, CoinGecko data shows.

However, that lead is narrowing hard and fast as crypto innovators continue to build more user-friendly frontends for crypto perp traders to interact with.

Hyperliquid was the breakthrough protocol, Infinex founder says

Decentralized perps platforms have been around for nearly a decade, led earlier by the likes of Synthetix, dYdX, and GMX. However, Hyperliquid was the first one to “get it right” and scale successfully, Infinex founder Kain Warwick told Cointelegraph earlier this month.

One of the most widely used crypto wallets, MetaMask, integrated Hyperliquid on Oct. 8, letting users access Hyperliquid’s perpetual swaps exchange directly through its app.

data.title: Nidec Corp. Shares Fall Sharply After It Retracts Earnings Guidance
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka

Nidec Corp. shares fell sharply after it retracted its earnings forecasts and decided not to pay an interim dividend, citing ongoing investigations into suspected accounting irregularities.

Shares were 6.4% lower at 2,387.0 yen, equivalent to $15.64, on Friday morning in Tokyo.

The Japanese maker of electric motors and other equipment said Thursday that its revenue and profit projections for the fiscal year ending March 2026 were undetermined and that it would announce them when it becomes possible to do so. Nidec also canceled a share buyback program announced in May.

The Kyoto-based manufacturer set up a third-party committee in September to investigate potential cases of improper accounting. The company said then that earlier probes had uncovered documents suggesting that the company might have engaged in improper accounting, including the potential cases related to the timing of asset write-downs.

Nidec had earlier projected revenue of Y2.600 trillion and net profit of Y200.00 billion for the fiscal year ending March 2026. It had planned to distribute an interim dividend of Y20 a share and buy back up to Y35.0 billion of its own shares under the repurchase program.

Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com

data.title: Japan Flash PMI Signals Further Slowdown in Private Sector — Market Talk
data.description: Flash PMI data for Japan points to a further loss of momentum across the private sector in October, says Annabel Fiddes, economics associate director at S&P Global Market Intelligence. Foreign demand for Japanese goods and services fell again, and new business declined for the first time in 16 months, S&P data show. While the service sector remained a key growth engine for the wider economy, fading momentum will be a point of concern to monitor in the coming months, Fiddes says. Manufacturing production continued to contract, but manufacturers were more upbeat about the year ahead than service providers. Many hope a recovery in global economic conditions, new product releases and stronger demand for electronics will help boost output, she adds.(fabiana.negrinochoa@wsj.com)

data.title: Alaska Air's Q3 revenue grows 23% YoY, hitting record high
data.description:
Overview

  • Alaska Air Group's Q3 2025 revenue reaches record $3.77 bln, up 23% YoY

  • Adjusted EPS for Q3 was $1.05, within prior expectations of $1 to $1.40

  • The carrier launched Atmos Rewards loyalty program, exceeding premium credit card sign-up goals

Outlook

  • Company expects Q4 unit revenue to increase by low single digits YoY

  • Alaska Air projects Q4 capacity growth of 2%-3% from last year

  • Company forecasts Q4 EPS of at least $0.40 and full-year EPS of at least $2.40

Result Drivers

  • REVENUE GROWTH - Record Q3 revenue driven by increased corporate travel and strong close-in demand

  • LOYALTY PROGRAM SUCCESS - Atmos Rewards launch exceeded premium credit card sign-up expectations

  • COST INCREASES - Higher fuel costs and elevated unit costs due to IT outage and challenging weather conditions

Key Details

Metric

Beat/Miss

Actual

Consensus Estimate

Q3 Revenue

$3.77 bln

Q3 Adjusted EPS

$1.05

Analyst Coverage

  • The current average analyst rating on the shares is "buy" and the breakdown of recommendations is 16 "strong buy" or "buy", no "hold" and no "sell" or "strong sell"

  • The average consensus recommendation for the airlines peer group is "buy"

  • Wall Street's median 12-month price target for Alaska Air Group Inc is $62.00, about 23.1% above its October 22 closing price of $47.68

  • The stock recently traded at 9 times the next 12-month earnings vs. a P/E of 10 three months ago

Press Release:
